message:
perf(pager): steal existing PageBuf on same-page write within a transaction

Repeated writes to the same page_no inside a single SimpleTransaction
previously allocated a fresh PageBuf from the pool for each
write_page/write_page_data call and then inserted it into the write_set,
which dropped the prior StagedPage (and returned its PageBuf to the
pool). For cursor-driven workloads — B-tree leaf restamps during bulk
INSERT/UPDATE, repeated DELETE cleanup on the same leaf — the pattern
is very common, and each round through the pool is a needless fence
pair even when the pool hits are free.

This commit introduces a same-page steal fast path:

- StagedPage::try_overwrite_bytes_in_place overwrites the underlying
  Buffered(PageBuf) or single-owner Owned(PageData) bytes in place,
  but ONLY when StagedPage::published has not been populated (no
  outstanding MVCC / cache reader holds a shared snapshot of the old
  bytes) and the length matches.

- write_page / write_page_data probe the write_set first; when an
  existing entry can be safely mutated, they skip the pool acquire
  entirely.

- A new PageData::is_single_owner_owned accessor lets the pager avoid
  accidentally triggering Arc::make_mut in the Owned arm — which would
  CoW into a fresh allocation and defeat the optimization.

Measured with a 10_000-iteration same-page rewrite microbenchmark
(new test_same_page_write_steals_existing_buffer in fsqlite-pager):
pool_hits and pool_misses both stay at zero across every repeated
write, and the new STAGED_PAGE_OVERWRITE_STEALS_TOTAL counter
records one steal per iteration. Before this change every one of
those iterations round-tripped a PageBuf through the pool.

comprehensive-bench (release-perf, --quick --filter insert/update,
best of 4 runs on a loaded workstation) shows results within
measurement noise (±5 %) vs. baseline — correctness-preserving, no
regression. The win is on the allocator + cache side: fewer page-sized
buffers churning through the pool and no redundant dereferences.

A second new test (test_same_page_write_after_publish_does_not_steal)
pins the safety invariant: once get_page has handed out a shared
snapshot, the following write MUST fall back to the allocate-fresh
path so the earlier snapshot stays stable — enforced by the
published.get().is_some() early return in
try_overwrite_bytes_in_place.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
